segments.s446-g89: ازان پس is a temporal phrase meaning "after that/thereupon." بفرمود is an honorific past verb used for a sovereign meaning "he commanded/said," and it appears before its subject here (a common narrative inversion). شاه اردشیر is the subject placed after the verb and names the speaker: "King Ardashir."
segments.s446-g90: که introduces the reported subordinate clause "that/that they..." کشتند is formally a 3rd-person plural past form "they killed," but in classical narrative after a royal command it functions to report an order (i.e. "that they kill him"); the subject is implicit. او را uses را as the definite/object marker attached after the pronoun. به is instrumental/means here ("by/with"), and باران تیر is a figurative genitive phrase "a shower/rain of arrows." Note the verb precedes the object, a flexible (poetic/classical) word order.
segments.s446-g49: This clause is a compact, poetic motion clause. فرامرز is the proper-name subject; پیش here is an adverb meaning “forward / in front,” placed before the verb for emphasis. آمدش is the past verb آمد with the enclitic ـش (third-person clitic) used in poetry to mark the action; با سپاه is a prepositional phrase “with (an) army” that complements the motion.
segments.s446-g50: جهان شد uses شد (past of شدن) as an intransitive/resultive verb: “the world became/was (so).” ز introduces the cause/agent (“from / because of”), followed by گرد سواران سیاه, a genitive phrase where گرد here means the dust or cloud raised by movement. Word order is poetically inverted but functions like a causal PP modifying شد.
segments.s446-g51: وزان is a fused prepositional/adverbial element “and/from that (sight),” anaphoric to the preceding scene. روی here means “sight/appearance” (literally “face”), followed by the proper name بهمن as the subject. صفی برکشید is a verbal phrase: برکشید (past) “raised/drew up” with صفی (a form relating to صف ‘a rank/line’) as its object; the clause thus means Bahman set up a formation.
segments.s446-g52: که introduces a result/explanatory clause: “so that / to the degree that.” خورشید تابان is the subject phrase “the shining sun.” زمین را is the direct object with the object marker را (here topicalizing the earth), and ندید is the past negative of دید “see” — literal order: “the shining sun did not see the earth.”
segments.s446-g53: ز آواز is a causal/ablative preposition phrase “from the sound (of).” شیپور و هندی form a coordinated noun phrase “trumpet and Indian …”; هندی is an adjectival/ethnic marker here (literary ‘Indian’/‘Indian-style’). درای in this phrase is not one of the glossed senses given and likely names a percussion instrument or drum; functionally the whole phrase means “from the sound of trumpet and Indian drum.”
segments.s446-g54: همی is an archaic/progressive particle used before the verb to give immediacy or iterative aspect (common in epic verse). کوه را uses را to topicalize/mark the mountain; دل is the inner part/‘heart’ of the mountain and is the grammatical subject of برآمد (“arose”/“issued forth”). ز جای is a prepositional phrase “from its place.” Literally: “indeed the mountain’s heart rose from its place.”
segments.s446-g55: بشست is a past transitive verb “washed” (V + subject inversion common in poetry), with آسمان as the subject. روی گیتی is the direct object phrase “the face of the world,” and به قیر is an instrumental/comitative phrase “with tar” — a darkening metaphor: the sky ‘washed’ the world’s face in tar.
segments.s446-g56: ببارید is the past of باریدن, intransitive “it rained / fell.” چون introduces a comparison “like/as,” and ژاله (dew) is the comparator. از introduces the source “from,” and ابر تیر is a genitive/compound image literally “cloud of arrows” (poetic), so the clause reads “it rained like dew from a cloud of arrows.”
segments.s446-g73: همه سربسر is an emphatic adverbial phrase: همه 'all/everyone' + سربسر 'entirely/wholly'. پشت برگاشتند is a past plural verb phrase: پشت 'back' (body) + برگاشتند (past, 3rd plural) 'they turned/returned (their) backs'. Together: 'everyone entirely turned their backs.' The verb is placed at the end in typical Persian order, and the subject is implicit in the plural verb.
segments.s446-g74: فرامرز را marks the definite direct object with را after the name Faramarz. خوار بگذاشتند is an idiomatic past-plural predicate: خوار 'despised/contemptible' + بگذاشتند (they left/made) — together meaning 'they treated (him) as despised' or 'they left him humiliated.' The verb is plural to agree with the implied plural agents.
segments.s446-g75: همه رزمگه: همه 'whole/entire' modifies رزمگه 'battlefield' (poetic noun phrase). کشته functions as a predicative plural/collective 'the slain' (no copula required in past descriptive clauses). چون کوه کوه is a simile: چون 'like' + repeated کوه 'mountain' (repetition gives the sense 'mountain upon mountain' or heaps). Word order is poetic but follows Persian patterns: modifiers before the head, simile after the predicate.
segments.s446-g76: به هم برفگنده is a prepositional/adjectival phrase describing how the slain lay: به هم 'onto one another/together' + برفگنده (an adjectival form meaning 'snow-laden' or 'heaped like snow' in this poetic usage) — together giving 'piled on one another.' ز هر دو گروه is a source phrase with the poetic preposition ز 'from' + هر دو گروه 'both groups/sides.' The whole phrase modifies the previous image of bodies piled like mountains.
segments.s446-g77: فرامرز is the proper-name subject. با اندکی رزمجوی is a prepositional phrase: با 'with' + اندکی 'a little/a few' (quantifier) + رزمجوی 'fighters/warriors' (singular form used collectively in poetry). The phrase means 'with a few warriors' and stands before the verb as an adjunct.
segments.s446-g78: به مردی introduces the opponent: به 'to/against' + مردی 'a man' (indefinite). به روی اندر آورد روی is a poetic idiom: به روی 'to (his) face' + اندر آورد (compound past, 'brought/turned in') + روی 'face' — literally 'brought his face to (the other's) face,' i.e. 'met face to face' or 'confronted him.' The verb آورد is past singular (he brought/turned), agreeing with the singular subject Faramarz.
segments.s446-g79: همه تنش is a possessive phrase: همه 'whole/entire' + تن 'body' + ـش (enclitic) 'his' = 'his whole body.' پر زخم شمشیر بود is a predicate: پر 'full (of)' + زخم 'wounds' + شمشیر 'of the sword' (noun-noun genitive relationship) + بود 'was' (past copula). The construction means 'his whole body was full of sword-wounds.'
segments.s446-g80: که introduces a explanatory relative/clausal remark: 'for he was ...'. فرزند شیران 'son/offspring of lions' is a metaphorical epithet meaning brave/fierce. The adjective بد here is poetic and functions as an intensifier meaning 'fierce' or 'terrible' (this sense is not one of the given dictionary senses), و شیر بود repeats the lion metaphor ('and a lion he was'), emphasizing his ferocity; بود is the past copula agreeing with the singular subject.
segments.s446-g41: چو + past verb (بشنید) here functions as a temporal clause 'when he heard'. پوشید is simple past 'he put on / donned'. The noun phrase خفتان جنگ follows پوشید and describes what he put on — a war‑garment/armour (poetic phrasing). Word order is typical older Persian: temporal clause → main verb → object.
segments.s446-g42: دلی پر ز کینه and سری پر ز ننگ are two parallel nominal phrases: 'a heart full of hatred' and 'a head full of shame'. پر ز ... is the poetic construction 'full of X' (پر = 'full (of)'; ز = 'of / from'). دلی and سری are the affected parts (heart, head). The phrases are juxtaposed without an explicit copula — typical of verse.
segments.s446-g43: سپه کرد uses a noun + کردن construction where کرد (past) makes 'spear/army' into the action 'he marshalled/gathered an army.' The coordinating و links the clauses. سر ... نهاد is a literal 'put/laid his head toward X' idiomatically meaning 'he turned toward / directed himself to Bahman'; نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put'.
segments.s446-g44: ز + noun (ز رزم ...) here means 'concerning / about' or 'from the battles of ...'. بسی functions adverbially 'much / many' and combines with کرد یاد ('made remembrance' = 'remembered') — an older periphrastic way to say 'he remembered many battles of Tahmatan.' Word order places the domain (ز رزم تهمتن) before the verb phrase بسی کرد یاد.
segments.s446-g45: چو + past (رسید) again = 'when ... arrived/reached.' نزدیک here qualifies the verb (approaching/near) and combines with the proper name بهمن to mean 'when the news reached Bahman' or 'when it drew near Bahman' (poetic compression). آگهی is 'news / information' — the item that 'reached.'
segments.s446-g46: برآشفت is an intransitive past meaning 'he became angry / was agitated' (the prefix بر- intensifies the change). The following prepositional phrase بر تخت شاهنشهی ('on the royal/imperial throne') states his location or the scene of his rising; note the verb precedes the locative phrase, a common poetic order.
segments.s446-g47: بنه برنهاد is a past verb phrase where برنهاد ('placed/raised') takes بنه as its object; here بنه functions as an instrument or object raised (in context: likely the standard/banner — a poetic or archaic word). The coordinating و links this to سپه برنشاند, where سپه (army) is the object and برنشاند (past) means 'set up / marshaled / placed in order' (he deployed the army).
segments.s446-g48: به غور اندر آمد is an idiom: به غور = 'into deep thought / into the depths', اندر آمد = 'he entered / came into'. The final clause دو هفته بماند uses the cardinal دو + هفته 'two weeks' and the present/subjunctive/optative‑style bماند (here past narrative sense 'he remained') to say he stayed for two weeks. Overall the verbs are narrative pasts; time phrase follows the verb.
segments.s446-g57: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' introducing the cause. The noun phrases follow the typical Persian noun-noun apposition: چاک تبرزین = 'the tearing/splitting of the battle-axe' (چاک as a noun 'tear, gash' qualified by تبرزین). و links the next noun phrase جر کمان = 'the tearing/snapping of the bow' (جر here is the noun 'ripping/tear'). Together they form the causal phrase 'because of the axes' splintering and bows' breaking.'
segments.s446-g58: زمین is the subject 'the earth/ground.' گشت is the past of گشتن used copularly 'became / turned (into).' جنبـان‌تر is a comparative adjective 'more agitated / more moving' (the -تر comparative). از آسمان is the standard comparison 'than the sky' (از + noun). Word order is Subject + Predicate + comparative phrase.
segments.s446-g59: سه روز و سه شب is a duration phrase 'three days and three nights.' هم here adds emphasis/continuity ('also / indeed / continuously') and برین رزمگاه is a locative phrase: بر (on/upon) + the demonstrative-like suffix ین (orthographic variant of این) giving 'on this/that battlefield' (poetic form). The whole segment means they remained/continued on that battlefield for the stated period.
segments.s446-g60: به here is instrumental/circumstantial 'by / in the manner of' (used adverbially). رخشنده روز = 'the radiant day' and تابنده ماه = 'the shining moon'; the two phrases are linked by و. The construction indicates the time / lighting conditions during those days and nights (i.e. both by day and by moonlight).
segments.s446-g61: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually / kept on' (poetic). گرز is the noun 'mace' (subject). بارید is the past 'rained (down)' used metaphorically of weapons falling. و links the second noun phrase پولاد تیغ, where پولاد 'steel' qualifies تیغ 'blade/sword' — together 'steel blades.' The segment lists the falling weapons in parallel.
segments.s446-g62: ز is the causal/from preposition 'from / because of.' گرد here is the noun 'dust' (ز گرد سپاه = 'from the dust of the host/array'). سپاه is 'army/host.' آسمان is the subject 'the sky.' گشت is 'became/turned' and میغ is a poetic noun 'blade' — the clause says the sky was transformed (figuratively) into a blade because of the host's dust (i.e. the scene seemed like a blade).
segments.s446-g63: به‌روز چهارم = 'on the fourth day' (به‌روز here 'of the day / on the day' in a temporal phrase). یکی 'one / a certain' introduces the following noun, باد 'wind.' خاست is the past 'arose / began' (archaic poetic past form). The segment thus gives the temporal setting and a sudden wind's rising.
segments.s446-g64: تو گفتی که is a reporting clause: تو (literal 'you') + گفتی (past 'said'); که introduces the reported content. In epic narration such addressing/reporting can be idiomatic; grammatically, گفتی is the past-tense verb 'said' and که introduces the subordinate clause. با روز شب گشت راست is an inverted word order: با روز (with/by the day) + شب گشت (night became/turned) + راست (an adverb 'truly / indeed' or 'right'). The clause means 'it was said / you said that the day had turned into night, truly.'
segments.s446-g25: نیای من is a possessive noun phrase (نیای + من) meaning 'my ancestor.' آن نامدار بلند is a noun phrase with the demonstrative آن followed by two adjectival modifiers (نامدار 'renowned' and بلند 'lofty/towering') describing the person who is the object of the verb in the next segment.
segments.s446-g26: گرفت is simple past 3rd-sg of گرفتن 'seize/take.' و coordinates the clauses. به زنجیر کردش is a causative/result phrase: به + زنجیر (chain) + کرد + ـش (3rd-sg object clitic) = 'he made/placed him in chains' (i.e. 'he shackled him'). به بند is a final result phrase 'into fetters / in bondage.'
segments.s446-g27: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). بودی is the past tense 'was/were' (here 'had been'). سپر پیش ایرانیان is a predicate nominal: سپر 'shield' with the prepositional phrase پیش ایرانیان 'before/in front of the Iranians' specifying the role/place.
segments.s446-g28: به مردی بهر کینه بسته میان: به here introduces the agent/causer ('by a man'); مردی is an indefinite 'a man.' بهر کینه 'for the sake of hatred' = 'out of spite,' and بسته is a past participle/adjective 'bound/tied.' میان 'in the midst / among' indicates the location ('bound in their midst'). The participle بسته agrees with the person described earlier in the clause.
segments.s446-g29: چه آمد is a rhetorical question/exclamation 'what has come/happened.' بدین is the prepositional combination (ب + this) meaning 'to this' — here written as the prefix ב plus the demonstrative element — and it links to نامور دودمان 'this famed lineage / house' which is the thing affected.
segments.s446-g30: که introduces the explanatory/result clause 'that ...' آید is the 3rd-sg present/future of آمدن 'comes/befalls.' ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'from every side,' بمابر is a dative compound meaning 'to/for us,' and زیان 'loss' is the thing that 'comes' — so the clause reads 'that loss comes upon us from every side.'
segments.s446-g31: پدر کشته is an absolute phrase 'father [was] slain' (کشته = past participle 'killed/slain'). و coordinates the next noun phrase. بند سایه نیا groups three nouns: بند 'fetter/bond', سایه 'shadow/protection' (here 'protection' is the intended meaning), and نیا 'the ancestor' (possessive relation: 'the protection/shadow of Nia' or 'Nia's protective bond').
segments.s446-g32: به مغز اندرون خون بود کیمیا is a locative+copular clause: به مغز اندرون 'in the marrow/innermost core,' خون 'the blood' is the topic, بود is past 'was,' and کیمیا 'alchemy/elixir' is a metaphorical predicate — 'his blood in the inner marrow was like an elixir.' Word order places location before the subject and predicate for poetic effect.
segments.s446-g65: 'به سوی فرامرز برگشت باد' = a prepositional phrase of direction followed by a verb and then the subject in poetic word order. 'به سوی فرامرز' means 'toward Farāmarz'; 'برگشت' (past) here = 'turned' or 'returned/was turned'; 'باد' is the subject 'the wind' placed after the verb (inversion common in epic Persian).
segments.s446-g66: 'جهاندار گشت از دم باد شاد' = a short clause with 'جهاندار' (the sovereign/one who holds the world) as subject and 'گشت' ('became') as the copular/predicative verb. 'از' introduces the cause/source ('from, by'); 'دم باد' = 'the breath/gust of the wind'; 'شاد' is the predicate adjective 'glad'. The order Subject–گشت–(cause phrase)–adjective is a compact poetic structure meaning 'the sovereign became glad because of the wind's gust.'
segments.s446-g67: 'همی شد پس گرد با تیغ تیز' begins with the epic particle 'همی' (continuous/performative) plus 'شد' ('happened/it came to pass'). 'پس' = 'then'; 'گرد' here functions as 'to go about / to wheel/turn' (verb) and 'با تیغ تیز' marks the instrument ('with a sharp sword'). The line therefore says that he turned/wheeled about brandishing a sharp sword.
segments.s446-g68: 'برآورد زان انجمن رستخیز' has the verb 'برآورد' (brought forth / raised / produced) followed by 'زان' = 'from that' and the noun phrase 'انجمن رستخیز' = 'that assembly of uprising/muster.' In poetic usage the verb precedes its object; the sense is 'he brought forth/raised an uprising from that gathering.'
segments.s446-g69: 'ز بستی و از لشکر زابلی' is a list of sources introduced by the poetic preposition 'ز'/'از' meaning 'from.' 'بستی' here is an archaic noun meaning a stronghold/fortress (poetic usage); 'و' = 'and'; 'لشکر زابلی' = 'the army of Zābul' (جابلی/زابلی as a demonym). The phrase enumerates forces coming from different places.
segments.s446-g70: 'ز گردان شمشیر زن کابلی' continues the listing: 'ز' = 'from'; 'گردان' = 'troop/unit'; 'شمشیر زن' is a compound meaning 'swordsman' (literally 'one who strikes with a sword' — here 'زن' functions as the agentive element); 'کابلی' = 'of Kabul / Kabul-born.' Together: 'from the troop of Kabul swordsmen.'
segments.s446-g71: 'برآوردگه بر سواری نماند' — 'برآوردگه' is the muster/place of reckoning; 'بر' = 'at/on' marks location; 'سواری' = 'riders, horsemen'; 'نماند' = 'remained (not)' (here negative: none were left). The clause means 'at the muster‑place no horsemen remained.'
segments.s446-g72: 'وزان سرکشان نامداری نماند' — 'وزان' = 'and from those' (linking back to the previous group); 'سرکشان' is the plural/poetic form meaning 'the rebellious ones' (arising from 'سرکش'); 'نامداری' = 'renown, fame'; 'نماند' = 'remained not.' The clause says that of those rebels no fame remained (they lost their renown).
segments.s446-g9: This is a subordinate clause introduced by که (that). بهمن is a proper name (subject); ز ما is a prepositional phrase 'from/us' marking the source or object of the verb that follows; کین اسفندیار is a noun phrase 'vengeance (against) Esfandiyar' functioning as the thing demanded. Word order is typical classical Persian: (که) + subject + prepositional source + object phrase.
segments.s446-g10: بخواهد is a 3rd-person verb (optative/indicative/future sense here: 'may/will want or demand') that completes the preceding 'that' clause. تو is the vocative/subject 'you' addressing the listener; این را is the definite object ('this' + object marker را). به بازی is an adverbial phrase 'as a game / as a jest'; مدار is an imperative/formulaic admonition meaning 'do not regard / do not treat' — here used in a poetic/archaic sense to tell the addressee not to make light of the matter.
segments.s446-g11: پدرم is the head noun 'my father' (پدر + possessive suffix). آن جهاندیده نامور are postpositive modifiers: آن 'that/the' (demonstrative), جهاندیده 'worldly‑wise', نامور 'renowned' — a string of epithets modifying the head noun in typical poetic order.
segments.s446-g12: ز گفت here means 'from the speech/words' (ز = 'from'); زواره is a proper name (the speaker or place) whose words are referenced. بپیچید سر is a past-tense verb phrase 'he turned/twisted his head' (ب- prefix + پیچید), where سر (head) is the direct object or the thing turned — together meaning he averted or turned his head in response to the speech.
segments.s446-g13: نپذرفت is a past tense verb 'he refused / did not accept'. The conjunction و links to نشنید, where نشنید is another past-tense negative 'he did not hear/heed'. اندرز او ('his advice') is the object of نشنید — word order places the object after the verb in this poetic line but the syntax is 'he did not heed his advice'.
segments.s446-g14: ازو 'from/of him / because of him' gives the cause; گشت is a past-tense copular/changes-of-state verb 'became'; ویران is the predicate adjective 'ruined, desolate'. کنون 'now' is a temporal adverb, and مرز او 'his border/frontier' is the nominal subject whose state is described. Literal order is causal phrase + verb + predicate + temporal + subject.
segments.s446-g15: نیا is a proper noun or title ('Niyā' / 'ancestor'); چون گذشت is a temporal clause 'when (he) passed/passed away' (چون used as 'when/as'); او is the subject 'he'. به شاهی رسید is a result clause: به + شاهی ('to kingship') + رسید ('he arrived/attained') — together 'he attained the throne.'
segments.s446-g16: This is a compact nominal clause: سر تاج شاهی is the phrase 'the crown (tāj) of kingship (šāhī)' with سر ('top/head') emphasizing the crown as placed on the head; به is the preposition 'onto / upon'; ماهی here is used poetically (a conventional epithet for a beautiful or exalted person/figure) and the verb رسید 'reached' completes the clause — 'the crown of kingship reached/settled upon his head.'
segments.s446-g81: سرانجام is an adverb meaning “at last/finally” placed at the clause front. بر + دست forms a prepositional phrase meaning “into/into the power of (the hand of...)” (دست here = control, hands). یاز appears immediately before the proper name اردشیر; in older Persian texts دست‌یاز(یدن) (‘to seize, lay hands on’) is implied, so this cluster indicates “into Ardashir’s hands.”
segments.s446-g82: گرفتار شد is a past passive/resultative construction: گرفتارشده > “was seized/was captured,” with شد functioning as an auxiliary. The two following words, نامدار دلیر, are postpositive epithets describing the (implicit) subject—literally “renowned, brave.” Adjectives follow the noun they qualify in this compact poetic order.
segments.s446-g83: بر here marks direction/toward and with the following proper name (بهمن) yields “to Bahman.” آوردش is the past tense of آوردن with the object clitic -ش (“him/it”) attached = “brought him.” از رزمگاه is a source phrase “from the battlefield,” with از preceding the place noun.
segments.s446-g84: بدو = به + او/او (here “to him/unto him”) and functions as the indirect object/adverbial “toward him.” کرد is the simple past “did/made” and here governs the object نگاه: کرد کین‌دار نگاه = “made (or cast) a vengeful look.” چندی is an adverb “for a while” modifying نگاه (“a look/glance”). Word order is compact: indirect object + verb + adjective + duration + noun.
segments.s446-g85: چو is the subordinating conjunction “when.” دیدش is the past of دیدن with the object clitic -ش (“he saw him”). ندادش is the negative past of دادن with the same clitic (“he did not give/spare him”); به جان is a fixed phrase literally “to/by life” (often used in oaths or to mean ‘spare for the sake of life’), and زینهار is an interjection/imperative-like exclamation “beware!/take heed!” — here the clause says that on seeing him he offered no mercy and the exclamation زینهار is present as a voiced admonition or formulaic cry.
segments.s446-g86: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning “he commanded/said.” داری here functions as an auxiliary with an infinitive to form a continuous/iterative sense “keep doing / continue to do,” and is followed by زدن (infinitive “to strike”). شهریار is the object “the king.” Thus the clause reports an order: “(He) commanded: continue striking the king.”
segments.s446-g87: فرامرز را: the proper name is marked by the definite object marker را. زنده precedes the verbal phrase to qualify the action (“alive”). بر دار کردن is the idiom “to put on the gallows / to hang,” with دار here meaning “gallows.” کرد is the simple past “did/made,” so زنده بر دار کرد = “he hanged (him) alive.”
segments.s446-g88: تن (“body”) is the theme; پیلوارش is a poetic adjective/qualifier attached to the body (in older usage it conveys a sense of successive/continuous suffering or condition), and نگونسار is an adjective meaning “mournful/woeful, pitiable.” کرد again is the past verb “made/did,” so the clause says he “made his body pitiable/a lamentable sight.” The compact word order places the descriptive cluster before the verb, typical of epic diction.
segments.s446-g17: کنون is a temporal adverb ‘now/presently’ placed before the noun phrase. The sequence بهمن نامور شهریار is a poetic name + epithets construction (name first, then adjectives/titles): ‘Bahman, famed (نامور) king (شهریار)’. This is a single noun phrase serving as the sentence’s subject.
segments.s446-g18: همی is a classical/perhaps archaic verbal particle here meaning ‘still/indeed’ or marking a habitual/continuous action; نو کند is a verb phrase ‘makes new / renews’ (نو = ‘new’, کند = auxiliary verb ‘does/makes’). کین‌شان is the object (kīn + possessive -شان) ‘their/against Esfandiyar’s enmity’ — so the verb phrase governs the object and the overall order is subject … (temporal) … verb phrase + object, typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s446-g19: هم acts as ‘also/too’ and از introduces source/reason (‘from’ / ‘because of’). The phrase کین مهر places two abstract nouns in succession (kīn = ‘hatred/vengeance’, mehr = ‘love/affection’), a poetic pairing meaning ‘from hatred and (from) love’ or ‘out of both hate and love’. آن سوار دلیر is an appositive noun phrase ‘that brave horseman’ and receives the force of the preceding prepositional phrase.
segments.s446-g20: ز (poetic form of از) again marks source ‘from’. نوش‌آذر is a proper name. آن گرد درنده شیر is a string of epic epithets in apposition: آن (‘that’), گرد — here an archaic epic noun meaning a warrior/chieftain (see note), درنده ‘ferocious/predatory’, شیر ‘lion’ (metaphorically ‘lion‑like fighter’). Together they form a clustered epithet referring back to the named source.
segments.s446-g21: کنون gives the time ‘now’. خواهد is the modal/future auxiliary ‘will/wants to’ (classical narrative future), followed by از ما ‘from us’ (the source or party affected). همی here functions as an adverb ‘also/indeed’ before the object. کین‌شان (‘their vengeance’) is the object marked by the possessive -شان and completes what he will exact or seek.
segments.s446-g22: به جای آورد is an idiomatic verbal unit: به (to/for) + جای (place) + آورد (bring) meaning ‘bring into place / restore / put into effect’. The coordinated object کین و آیین‌شان (‘their vengeance and their rites/customs’) follows the verb; آیین‌شان uses the possessive suffix -شان. The verb thus takes a compound object (کین و آیین‌شان).
segments.s446-g23: ز ایران سپاهی is a prepositional phrase ‘an army from Iran’ (ز = poetic از ‘from’, ایران = Iran, سپاهی = army/warrior(s) used here collectively). چو introduces a simile ‘like/as’, followed by ابر سیاه ‘a black cloud’ — the standard poetic comparison placing the simile after the noun it modifies.
segments.s446-g24: بیاورد is the verb ‘(he) brings’ (present/future narrative). نزدیک ما is a locative/benefactive phrase ‘near us / to our vicinity’ and functions adverbially. کینه‌خواه is a noun ‘avenger’ or ‘one who seeks vengeance’ and is the direct object brought near; word order places the verb before its adverbial and object in this poetic line.
segments.s446-g33: The phrase 'به تاراج داده' is a classical arrangement meaning 'has given to pillage' (preposition + noun + perfect verb). Here داده is a past participle/finite perfect 'given' and همه مرز خویش is its object: 'all his borders'. The enclitic ش on خوی indicates possession ('his'); the preposition به + تاراج expresses 'into/for pillage'.
segments.s446-g34: نبینم is a negative present/present-subjunctive form meaning 'I do not see / I see not' (classical short negative without می). 'سر مایه' functions as the compound 'سرمایه' = 'capital, chief resource', followed by ارز 'value/wealth' and the possessive خویش 'his' (sh enclitic). The noun group is the object of نبینم: 'I do not see the capital/treasure of his wealth.'
segments.s446-g35: شما (you, plural/formal) is the subject; نیز 'also' adds inclusiveness. یکسر is an adverbial intensifier 'entirely/at once'; چه introduces the question 'what' and گویید is the 2nd-person-plural present (or classical subjunctive/imperative) 'you say' used in an interrogative sense. باز at the end means 'again/still' and modifies the whole question. Word order places adverbs before the verb in this classical register.
segments.s446-g36: هرانکس is the fused indefinite 'هر آن کس' meaning 'whoever/whichever person'; که is the relative/linking particle 'who/that' introducing the clause; هستید is the 2nd-person-plural present 'you are'; گردن‌فراز is a descriptive epithet 'proud' used here as a vocative or predicate addressing the addressees. The clause means 'whoever you are, (you) proud ones.'
segments.s446-g37: بگفتند is the simple past plural 'they said'. کای is a vocative particle meaning 'O' (and can also appear as the dynastic title Kay); گرد روشن‌روان is the addressee: a proper name or title (گرد) followed by the epithet روشن‌روان 'bright‑souled'. Together they form a direct address ('O Gird, bright‑souled').
segments.s446-g38: The doubled phrase پدر بر پدر بر is a poetic intensification: each پدر 'father/ancestor' is preceded by the preposition بر 'upon/on', yielding a sense like 'father upon father' (an emphatic blessing or encomium). توی is the dative/locative poetic form 'to/for you', and پهلوان is a vocative 'O champion/hero'. Syntactically this addresses the hero with the repeated blessing: 'father upon father (be) to you, O champion.'
segments.s446-g39: همه یک به یک is an idiomatic adverbial 'all, one by one / each in turn'. پیش تو is a locative/prepositional phrase 'before you / in your presence'. بنده‌ایم is the first‑person‑plural predicative 'we are servants' (ایم = we are), used here as a humble formula 'we are your servants.'
segments.s446-g40: برای introduces purpose or cause 'for / on behalf of'; و simply links clauses 'and'; به فرمان تو is an agentive/instrumental phrase 'by your command' (به + فرمان + possessive تو); زنده‌ایم is the first‑person‑plural 'we live' / 'we are alive'. Together: 'For you and by your command we live.'
segments.s446-g1: غمی شد is a literary past construction meaning “sorrow befell / sorrow came” (شد = ‘became’). The patient/subject فرامرز follows the verb in inverted epic word order. در مرز بست is a second predication whose verb بست (past of بستن) agrees with فرامرز: بست = “he closed / shut (up)”; در مرز = “at/on the frontier.” The two clauses are sequential actions of the same subject.
segments.s446-g2: ز is the poetic form of از (“from/of”); در دنیا = “in the world.” دست کین را is a definite object phrase (“the hand of vengeance”) with را marking the object. بشست is the past tense of شستن (“he washed”). The idiom دستِ کین را بشستن = “to wash one’s hand of vengeance” (i.e. renounce or cast off revenge). Word order places the object before the verb, which is normal in Persian.
segments.s446-g3: همه is a universal quantifier (“all/everyone”) placed before the noun. نامداران is a plural substantive meaning “the famed/renowned ones,” and روشن‌روان is a compound epithet (“bright‑souled / clear‑soul”) modifying them. The whole phrase is a nominal group meaning “all the famed, bright‑souled men.”
segments.s446-g4: برفتند is past‑plural “they went / departed.” یکسر here is an adverbial meaning “at once / all together,” modifying their movement. بر (here = به) is the directional preposition “to/toward,” and پهلوان is the destination noun “the champion/hero.” The subject is implicit in the verb (they = the men just mentioned).
segments.s446-g5: بدان is an archaic/dialectal literary form corresponding to به + آن = “to those.” It marks the indirect object/recipients (نامداران). زبان برگشاد is a literary idiom: زبان (“tongue / speech”) + برگشاد (past participle of گشودن, “opened”) = “opened his mouth / began to speak.” Word order places the dative/recipient (بدان نامداران) before the predication.
segments.s446-g6: ز = از (“from/of”) introduces the source or domain (“in/from his saying”). گفت here functions as a noun meaning “what was said / the speech.” ز گفت = “in his speech.” زواره is a proper name (Zovāreh) that is the theme of mention. بسی is a classical adverb meaning “much / many (times)” modifying کرد یاد, where کرد is a light verb “did/made” and یاد is “remembrance / mention” — together meaning “made much mention of Zovāreh.” The order (theme before the light‑verb phrase) is typical of older Persian.
segments.s446-g7: که is the relative/complementizer “which/that” introducing the content of what was said. پیش پدرم = “before / in the presence of my father,” where پدرم is پدر + possessive suffix ـم (“my father”). آن جهاندیده مرد is a nominal phrase: آن = “that,” جهاندیده = “worldly‑wise / experienced,” مرد = “man.” The whole phrase identifies the speaker (‘that experienced man’ in front of my father).
segments.s446-g8: همی گفت combines همی (a durative/habitual particle, “kept/used to”) with گفت (past) to give a habitual sense: “he would say / he used to say.” The conjunction و links the next clause. لبها پر از بادسرد is a nominal predicate without an explicit copula (common in poetry): لبها (“the lips”) + پر (“full”) + از + بادسرد (“of cold wind / chill”) = “his lips were full of a cold wind” (a poetic way to describe a chill or tremulous voice).

sentences.s446-s45: After that, King Ardashir ordered that they kill him with a shower of arrows.
sentences.s446-s25: Faramarz advanced forward with an army; the world became covered by the dust of black horsemen.
sentences.s446-s26: And at that sight Bahman drew up a battle-line so that the shining sun could not see the earth.
sentences.s446-s27: From the sound of trumpets and the Indian (tambourine) they began — indeed the mountain’s heart rose from its place.
sentences.s446-s28: The sky washed the face of the world with tar; it rained like dew from a cloud of arrows.
sentences.s446-s37: Everyone completely turned their backs; they left Faramarz humiliated.
sentences.s446-s38: The entire battlefield was strewn with the slain, piled mountain upon mountain from both groups.
sentences.s446-s39: Faramarz, with a few warriors, confronted a man and came face to face with him.
sentences.s446-s40: His whole body was full of sword-wounds, for he was a son of lions — fierce and lion-like.
sentences.s446-s21: When he heard it, he donned the garments of war; his heart was full of hatred, his head full of shame.
sentences.s446-s22: He mustered an army and turned his head toward Bahman; he recalled many battles of Tahmatan.
sentences.s446-s23: When the news reached Bahman, he was enraged and rose onto the imperial throne.
sentences.s446-s24: He raised the standard and set the army in motion; he fell into deep thought and remained for two weeks.
sentences.s446-s29: From the splitting of battle-axes and the tearing of bows, the earth became more agitated than the sky.
sentences.s446-s30: For three days and three nights on that battlefield — by the radiant day and by the shining moon.
sentences.s446-s31: Maces rained down and steel blades; from the dust of the host the sky turned into a blade.
sentences.s446-s32: On the fourth day a wind arose; it was said that the day had truly turned to night.
sentences.s446-s13: My ancestor seized that famed, towering one and bound him in chains.
sentences.s446-s14: who had been a shield before the Iranians, bound in the midst by a man's spite.
sentences.s446-s15: What has befallen this famed house, that from every side loss should come upon us?
sentences.s446-s16: Father slain and Nia's protective bond bound; in the inner marrow his blood was an elixir.
sentences.s446-s33: A wind turned toward Farāmarz, and the sovereign became glad at the gust of the wind.
sentences.s446-s34: Then he wheeled about with a sharp sword, and from that assembly he raised an uprising.
sentences.s446-s35: From the fort, and from the Zābul army, from the troop of Kabul swordsmen.
sentences.s446-s36: At the muster‑place there remained no riders, and of those rebels no renown remained.
sentences.s446-s5: That Bahman may demand from us vengeance on Esfandiyar; you, do not treat this as a game.
sentences.s446-s6: My father, that worldly‑wise renowned man, turned his head away from Zovareh's words.
sentences.s446-s7: He refused and did not heed his counsel; because of him his frontier has now become desolate.
sentences.s446-s8: When Niyā had passed away, he attained kingship; the crown of sovereignty came upon his head.
sentences.s446-s41: At last the renowned brave fell into Ardashir's hands.
sentences.s446-s42: He brought him from the battlefield to Bahman, and for a while cast at him a vengeful glance.
sentences.s446-s43: When he saw him he gave him no mercy; (shouting) “Zinehār!” he commanded, “keep striking the king.”
sentences.s446-s44: He put Farāmarz up alive on the gallows and made his body a pitiable sight.
sentences.s446-s9: Now Bahman, the famed king, renews the feud against Esfandiyar.
sentences.s446-s10: Also, from hatred and love that brave horseman; from Nush‑Āzar that fierce, lion‑like chieftain.
sentences.s446-s11: Now he will exact from us their vengeance and restore their vengeance and rites.
sentences.s446-s12: From Iran he brings an army like a black cloud, an avenger coming near to us.
sentences.s446-s17: He has given all his borderlands to pillage; I see not the capital, the treasure of his wealth.
sentences.s446-s18: And you as well — what do you all now say? Whoever you are, proud ones.
sentences.s446-s19: They said, 'O Gird of bright soul! Father upon father be upon you, O champion.'
sentences.s446-s20: All of us, one by one, are your servants before you; we live for you and by your command.
sentences.s446-s1: Sorrow seized Faramarz; he shut himself up at the border and washed his hand of vengeance from the world.
sentences.s446-s2: All the renowned, bright‑souled men went at once to the champion.
sentences.s446-s3: To those renowned men he opened his tongue; in his speech he made much mention of Zovāreh.
sentences.s446-s4: which that worldly‑wise man used to say in my father's presence, and his lips were full of a cold wind.
